description: An animal is 'woenu' when familiarity has accustomed it to human presence or handling. Tameness belongs to an individual animal's present relation with people, whereas domestication describes the longer history of a population. 'moli' (gentle) and 'shurano' (safe) remain separate judgements.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into true-mid rounded back 'o'. Release the rounding and move into a separate stressed true-mid front 'e', keeping the 'o.e' hiatus clear. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and finish with high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The first two syllables share the open 'wo.e' contour of 'woeli' (natural), but the final dental nasal closes the distance before rounded 'u'. That settling motion suggests an animal remaining nearby instead of withdrawing.
usage_notes: The adjective normally applies to an animal, as in 'woenu nolika' (tame animal). People are better described through the actions and relations they choose.

semantic_domains.community: an individual animal's accustomed proximity to people
semantic_domains.nature: animal behaviour changed through familiarity with human presence or handling
